title: add multi-arch build of upstream skopeo image via Travis
body:
This PR is connected to https://github.com/containers/skopeo/issues/1010 to get skopeo multi-arch container images.
 
To build multi-arch upstream container image Travis is used, as it has native hardware to run the build for many architectures (amd64, s390x, ppc64le). Docker is used as build and manifest tool. `quay.io/skopeo/upstream:master`, `quay.io/skopeo/stable:v1.2.0` and `quay.io/containers/skopeo:v1.2.0` are specified as target multi-arch images.
Travis config file has 3 stages:
- `local-build` to do the local binary build and test for linux/amd64 and osx, as it was in
the initial code
- `image-build-push` to build and push images for specific architectures (amd64, s390x, ppc64le)
- `multi-arch-manifest-push` to create and push manifest for multi-arch image - `quay.io/skopeo/upstream:master`, `quay.io/skopeo/stable:v1.2.0` and `quay.io/containers/skopeo:v1.2.0`

2 last stages are not done for pull request event or for non-master branch.

2 env variables in Travis are expected - QUAY_USERNAME and QUAY_PASSWORD to push the images to quay.io. 
As a result multi-arch image for 3 architectures is published.

The code is tested using another quay registry to push https://travis-ci.com/github/barthy1/skopeo/builds/194396214

Signed-off-by: Yulia Gaponenko <yulia.gaponenko1@de.ibm.com>

comments.body: @rhatdan thanks for the answer. I've created this PR to get understanding if this approach is acceptable. If it's ok, I can do the same for stable branch. Just want to go step by step :)
@barthy1 I want multi arch images available in quay.io and to use manifests correctly.  So I am fine with this.  I want to get feedback @mtrmac @vrothberg @TomSweeneyRedHat though.

I love the concept, but unfortunately, I've been buried in other stuff the past couple of days.  I do want to dig into this though.
@edsantiago @cevich if you've a spare cycle or three to eyeball, input would be appreciated.
@TomSweeneyRedHat I've updated the PR, adding image build for stable version and pushing it to `quay.io/containers/skopeo:ma` and `quay.io/skopeo/stable:ma`
@TomSweeneyRedHat any suggestions for this PR?
Thanks for the ping @barthy1 .  @edsantiago or @cevich, could one of you take a looksee at this please?

I will not have time to look this week. My recollection from last month is that I had a strong averse reaction to the `:ma` : I think it is not futureproof. Is there any reason not to just build and push a multiarch `skopeo:version`?
@barthy1 That works for me.

@edsantiago 

> Did you look for ways to refactor the multiple image-build-push stanzas? Maybe using with_items? I worry that the duplication will become unmaintainable.

Iterating via archs is not doable in case when we need to keep order and archs should be applied only to one stage.
I just refactored a little bit the code to have `script` for image-build-push in one place.
@edsantiago hey! any time this week to review the PR?

Suggestion 1:  Since both the skopeo binary build and the container images follow after package updates, it probably makes sense to enable some cron-triggered (in Travis settings) builds.  Otherwise the binaries/images could miss out on important dependency security updates if the pace of PR merges (as written) aren't rapid enough.

Suggestion 2:  (forgive me if I missed it) It would be helpful to maintainers, if just the "build" steps also ran for PRs (i.e. only skip the push).  This way any code/script changes which affect the mechanics will be noticed right away, instead of being delayed to branch-push time.

Quick note first: the CI failure is not your PR's fault; it is being investigated in #1096.

I've been trying to review this today, but it's a big job for me because I don't know anything about Travis. It's now late and my brain is fried; and I'm still not much closer to understanding the full details of this PR. I'm sorry.

@cevich thank you for suggestions!
> Suggestion 1: Since both the skopeo binary build and the container images follow after package updates, it probably makes sense to enable some cron-triggered (in Travis settings) builds. Otherwise the binaries/images could miss out on important dependency security updates if the pace of PR merges (as written) aren't rapid enough.

Good point. You can enable Travis cron jobs in your Travis project settings - https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/cron-jobs/ Nothing is configured in the code. 

> Suggestion 2: (forgive me if I missed it) It would be helpful to maintainers, if just the "build" steps also ran for PRs (i.e. only skip the push). This way any code/script changes which affect the mechanics will be noticed right away, instead of being delayed to branch-push time.

That's just brilliant! You are absolute right, sorry that I didn't do that from the beginning. Now I've added image builds (upstream and stable) to PR pipeline. That's you can see already in Travis setup for my PR - https://travis-ci.org/github/containers/skopeo/builds/741935428 . The push part won't be done, as it's still disabled for PR case.

> You can enable Travis cron jobs in your Travis project settings

Yep, I'm happy to set this up.

> That's just brilliant! You are absolute right, sorry that I didn't do that from the beginning.

You wouldn't be saying that if you knew how many years it took me to arrive at that :disappointed: 

Presumably w/o much/any effort, this will also be covered by cron-triggered builds.  I believe they simply execute as if branch-push happened, so the full build + push workflow should happen.

Sorry my Travis-Foo isn't what it use to be

> Suggestion 2: (forgive me if I missed it) It would be helpful to maintainers, if just the "build" steps also ran for PRs (i.e. only skip the push). This way any code/script changes which affect the mechanics will be noticed right away, instead of being delayed to branch-push time.

That’s definitely useful, OTOH I’m worried about the update-test cycle getting longer; it’s already too long for much comfort. Is there a way to measure the time spent running the tests but not waiting for Travis to queue the first one?

Travis reports “total time”, which just seems to sump the duration of all tasks, and probably does not account for parallelism; and a “Ran for” which I can’t interpret (it is now shorter than before, at least in one instance?!), so neither seems to be a
I am going to merge, and we can look at the Podman versus Docker in a different PR.

@rhatdan thank you for merging! Don't forget to specify QUAY_USERNAME and QUAY_PASSWORD in Travis settings for this repo -> https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/environment-variables/#defining-variables-in-repository-settings
@TomSweeneyRedHat Do you know what these are?

Just in case - `QUAY_USERNAME` and `QUAY_PASSWORD` are used to push container images to `quay.io` registry. Usually you can get user and password for registry login via `quay.io` robot account -> https://docs.quay.io/glossary/robot-accounts.html
For skopeo case you need robot account with write permissions to `quay.io/skopeo` and `quay.io/containers`

> Don't forget to specify QUAY_USERNAME and QUAY_PASSWORD

@rhatdan I know what they are, I'll see if I can set them...

...rats.  Two major problems:

1. I don't have access to either `skopeo` or `containers`, @TomSweeneyRedHat can you invite me?
2. Quay hard-codes the repository name as a prefix to the robot user name.  i.e. It's impossible to use the same account for both `skopeo` and `containers` :disappointed:

...double checked.  Indeed, the robot accounts are all namespaced to the user or orginization account that owns them.  There's no way around this :cry:   i.e. no matter what "name" I give the robot, quay will create `skopeo+name` and `containers+name` :sob: 

I'll open up a PR to split `QUAY_USERNAME` and `QUAY_PASSWORD` across the two quay namespaces.  I'm pretty sure that's the only option.  Once I get permissions to actually create those robot accounts, I'll setup the env. vars. in travis.
Opened https://github.com/containers/skopeo/pull/1104
Lol @TomSweeneyRedHat okay, why not.  Yes, I'm happy to manage this stuff as I've got my fingers in all the automation pies anyway.  Yummy.
